Excerpt from Chapter 9 of Roll of Thunder, Hear My Cry
(1) When supper was ready, I eagerly grabbed the iron bell before Christopher-John or Little Man could claim it, and ran onto the back porch to summon Papa, Mr. Morrison, and Stacey from the fields. As the three of them washed up on the back porch, Mama went to the end of the porch where Papa stood alone. "What did Mr. Jamison want?" she asked, her voice barely audible.

(2) Papa took the towel Mama handed him, but did not reply immediately. I was just inside the kitchen dipping out the butter beans. I moved closer to the window so that I could hear his answer.

(3) "Don't keep anything from me, David. If there's trouble, I want to know."

(4) Papa looked down at her. "Nothing to worry 'bout, honey ....just seems that Thurston Wallace been in town talking 'bout how he's not gonna let a few smart colored folks ruin his business. Says he's gonna put a stop to this shopping in Vicksburg. That's all."

(5) Mama sighed and stared out across the plowed field to the sloping pasture land. "I'm feeling scared, David," she said.

(6) Papa put down the towel. "Not yet, Mary. It's not time to be scared yet. They're just talking."

(7) Mama turned and faced him. "And when they stop talking?"

(8) "Then ...then maybe it'll be time. But right now, pretty lady," he said, leading her by the hand toward the kitchen door, "right now I've got better things to think about."

(9) Quickly I poured the rest of the butter beans into the bowl and hurried across the kitchen to the table. As Mama and Papa entered, I slid onto the bench beside Little Man and Christopher-John. Papa beamed down at the table. "Well, look-a-here!" he exclaimed. "Good ole butter beans and cornbread! You better come on, Mr. Morrison! You too, son!" he called. "These womenfolks done gone and fixed us a feast."

(10) After school was out, spring drooped quickly toward summer; yet Papa had not left for the railroad. He seemed to be waiting for something, and I secretly hoped that whatever that something was, it would never come so that he would not leave. But one evening as he, Mama, Big Ma, Mr. Morrison, and Stacey sat on the front porch while Christopher-John, Little Man, and I dashed around the yard chasing fireflies, I overheard him say, "Sunday I'm gonna have to go. Don't want to though. I got this gut feeling it ain't over yet. It's too easy."

(11) I released the firefly imprisoned in my hand and sat beside Papa and Stacey on the steps. "Papa, please," I said, leaning against his leg, "don't go this year."

(12) Stacey looked out into the falling night, his face resigned, and said nothing.

(13) Papa put out his large hand and caressed my face. "Got to, Cassie girl," he said softly. "Baby, there's bills to pay and ain't no money coming in. Your mama's got no job come fall and there's the mortgage and next year's taxes to think of."

(14) "But, Papa, we planted more cotton this year. Won't that pay the taxes?"

(15) Papa shook his head. "With Mr. Morrison here we was able to plant more, but that cotton is for living on; the railroad money is for the taxes and the mortgage."

(16) I looked back at Mama wanting her to speak, to persuade him to stay, but when I saw her face I knew that she would not. She had known he would leave, just as we all had known.

(17) "Papa, just another week or two, couldn't you—"

(18) "I can't, baby. May have lost my job already."

(19) "But Papa—"

(20) "Cassie, that's enough now," Mama said from the deepening shadows.

(21) I grew quiet and Papa put his arms around Stacey and me, his hands falling casually over our shoulders. From the edge of the lawn where Little Man and Christopher-John had ventured after lightning bugs, Little Man called, "Somebody's coming!" A few minutes later Mr. Avery and Mr. Lanier emerged from the dusk and walked up the sloping lawn. Mama sent Stacey and me to get more chairs for the porch, then we settled back beside Papa still sitting on the steps, his back propped against a pillar facing the visitors.

(22) "You goin' up to the store tomorrow, David?" Mr. Avery asked after all the amenities had been said. Since the first trip in January, Mr. Morrison had made one other trip to Vicksburg, but Papa had not gone with him.

(23) Papa motioned to Mr. Morrison. "Mr. Morrison and me going the day after tomorrow. Your wife brought down that list of things you need yesterday."

(24) Mr. Avery cleared his throat nervously. "It's—it's that list I come 'bout, David. ...I don't want them things no more."

(25) The porch grew silent.

(26) When no one said anything, Mr. Avery glanced at Mr. Lanier, and Mr. Lanier shook his head and continued. "Mr. Granger making it hard on us, David. Said we gonna have to give him sixty percent of the cotton, 'stead of fifty ...now that the cotton's planted and it's too late to plant more. ...Don't s'pose though that it makes much difference. The way cotton sells these days, seems the more we plant, the less money we gets anyways—"

(27) Mr. Avery's coughing interrupted him and he waited patiently until the coughing had stopped before he went on. "I'm gonna be hard put to pay that debt in Vicksburg, David, but I'm gonna. ...I want you to know that." ...

(28) Mr. Avery's coughing started again and for a while there was only the coughing and the silence. But when the coughing ceased, Mr. Lanier said, "I pray to God there was a way we could stay in this thing, but we can't go on no chain gang, David."

(29) Papa nodded. "Don't expect you to, Silas."

(30) Mr. Avery laughed softly. "We sure had 'em goin' for a time though, didn't we?"

(31) "Yes," agreed Papa quietly, "we sure did."

(32) When the men had left, Stacey snapped, "They got no right pulling out! Just 'cause them Wallaces threaten them one time they go jumping all over themselves to get out like a bunch of scared jackrabbits—"

(33) Papa stood suddenly and grabbed Stacey upward. "You, boy, don't you get so grown you go to talking 'bout more than you know. Them men, they doing what they've gotta do. You got any idea what a risk they took just to go shopping in Vicksburg in the first place? They go on that chain gang and their families got nothing. They'll get kicked off that plot of land they tend and there'll be no place for them to go. You understand that?"
This explains that you don't always have to give up on things, you can keep doing them, even if you can't.

However, the answer does not clearly connect the examples of space exploration to the idea that if humanity can do difficult things like reach the moon, then providing education should also be possible. The key rhetorical point ('if we can do X, we can do Y') is not fully stated. The response is text-specific and not generic, so it is not incorrect, but it does not meet the threshold for 'correct.'

responses.content: give children quality education
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'give children quality education' directly answers the question about Malala's point in using space exploration as an example. The student identifies that Malala is arguing that if humanity can achieve difficult things like reaching the moon, then providing quality education for all children should also be possible. This matches the rhetorical purpose of the text and meets both the form and accuracy requirements for the 'correct' bucket.

questions.question: Which of the following most closely reflects the argument of this section of the speech?

responses.content: B. All children deserve access to education regardless of where they live.

questions.question: Which of the following claims from the speech does NOT specifically contribute to the argument identified in the previous question?

responses.content: D. “Not just the politicians and the world leaders, we all need to contribute.”

activities.text_title: Freedom Walkers: The Story of the Montgomery Bus

questions.question: Reread paragraph 6. What is the closest meaning of “she must have been paying attention in her civics classes” as it is used in the text?

responses.content: they got arrested
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'they got arrested' directly answers the question about what happened to Edwina and Marshall Johnson when they refused to give up their bus seats. The text states they were arrested, which matches the student's answer. The answer is brief but accurate and aligns with the key scene elements. There is no contradiction or fabrication.

questions.question: According to paragraph 13, how did Claudette respond when the police asked if she was going to get up?

responses.content: A. Colvin was seen as a model defendant for the NAACP to challenge segregation laws because of what a good student she was.
C. Colvin was well-versed in the law before refusing to give up her seat on the bus.

questions.question: Based on paragraph 22, what were civil rights leaders looking for before they challenged bus segregation in federal court?

responses.content: she did not get up
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'she did not get up' shows that the student understands Claudette refused to move, which is a key part of the scene. However, it does not include Claudette's verbal assertion of her rights or her reasoning, which are important details from the text. The answer is text-specific and not generic, but it lacks the specificity required for a 'correct' classification.

responses.content: She said she would not get up because she knew her rights
responses.bucket_rationale: The response shows text-specific engagement by mentioning that Claudette refused to get up and referenced her rights. However, it lacks the specific details from the text, such as her direct quote ('No,' 'I don't have to get up,' 'It's my constitutional right'). The answer is accurate in spirit but does not fully meet the threshold for 'correct' because it is missing the specific language and reasoning Claudette used. Therefore, 'partial-understanding' is the best fit.

responses.content: it states that 'No, I don't have to get up. I paid my fare, so I don't have to get up."
responses.bucket_rationale: The response accurately quotes Claudette's refusal and her reasoning ('No, I don't have to get up. I paid my fare, so I don't have to get up.'), which matches the key scene elements from paragraph 13. The answer is text-specific, demonstrates understanding of her defiance, and does not contradict the text. The student has moved from a vague answer to a specific, accurate one, meeting the threshold for 'correct.'

questions.question: In paragraph 21, Jo Ann Robinson said the verdict was a “bombshell.” What does she mean by this?

responses.content: they were looking for  freedom
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'they were looking for freedom' shows some engagement with the text and the general theme of the civil rights movement, but it does not specifically address the requirement that leaders were looking for a person of strong character and reputation to be the face of the case. The answer is vague and lacks the specificity needed for a 'correct' classification, but it is not off-topic or incorrect, so 'partial-understanding' is appropriate.

responses.content: they needed a white person
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'they needed a white person' directly contradicts the text. The text states that civil rights leaders were looking for a black rider who was above reproach, with unassailable character and reputation, not a white person. This is a clear misunderstanding of the key requirement described in paragraph 22.

responses.content: they needed the strongest possible case
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'they needed the strongest possible case' directly matches the key element from the text in paragraph 22. The student identifies that civil rights leaders were looking for the strongest possible case before going to federal court, which aligns with the requirement for a person of unassailable character and reputation. The response is concise but accurate and meets the threshold for 'correct.'

questions.question: What is a central idea from Chapter 2 of Freedom Walkers?

responses.content: A. Black leaders in Montgomery had no idea how they would challenge the bus laws.

questions.question: Which sentence from the text most strongly supports the correct answer to the previous question?

responses.content: B. “She had expected to be cleared, and when the judge announced his verdict, she broke into agonized sobs that shook everyone in the crowded courtroom.”

activities.text_title: Freedom’s Daughters: The Unsung Heroines of the Civil Rights Movement from 1830 to 1970

questions.question: How was Barbara Johns similar to her uncle Vernon?

responses.content: that it is racist
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'that it is racist' directly identifies the discriminatory and segregationist attitudes of the Farmville school district, which aligns with the key scene elements in the text. The text describes systematic racial segregation and unfair treatment of black residents and students, so the response is accurate and meets the threshold for 'correct.'

questions.question: What inspired Barbara Johns to fight for a better school?

responses.content: because her and her uncle did the same thing like trying to give people rights
responses.bucket_rationale: The response identifies that both Barbara Johns and her uncle Vernon were involved in trying to give people rights, which aligns with the text's depiction of them as crusaders for civil rights and willing to act against injustice. The answer is in the student's own words and demonstrates understanding of their shared activist nature.

questions.question: As used in paragraph 6, what does the word “ploy” mean?

responses.content: because they were made tar paper and too hot and then also turned too cold, and the whites had pretended to promise the blacks something better but never did.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response mentions the poor conditions of the school (tar paper, too hot, too cold) and the broken promises from the white school district, both of which are key elements from the text that inspired Barbara Johns to take action. The answer is specific and text-based, meeting the threshold for 'correct.'

questions.question: Why did Barbara Johns decide to plan a student boycott?

responses.content: B. A clever trick or scheme to deceive someone

questions.question: Which of the following statements best conveys the central idea of this text?

responses.content: C. After the adults in her community failed to accomplish any change, Barbara Johns led fellow African American students in a strike for better school conditions.

questions.question: Which sentence from the excerpt best supports the correct answer to Question 7?

responses.content: B. “Joined by the other student organizers, she called for a student strike and produced hand-lettered picket signs proclaiming 'We Want a New School or None at All' and 'Down with the Tar-Paper Shacks.'”
